description: Phase 1 — Core value features: data richness, UX power, and first whimsy hook

userStories.title: OpenLibrary ISBN auto-fill in Add Book form
userStories.description: As a user, I want to type an ISBN and have the book's title, author, publisher, and description auto-populated from OpenLibrary, so I don't have to type everything manually.
acceptanceCriteria: Add an 'Lookup' button next to the ISBN field in the Add Book form
Clicking the button calls GET /api/books/openlibrary/{isbn} with the current ISBN value
On success, title, author first/last name, and description fields are populated from the response
Button shows a loading state while the request is in flight
If the ISBN is not found, show a non-blocking inline error message near the ISBN field
If the ISBN field is empty, the button is disabled
All existing form validation and submission behaviour is unchanged
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
Backend builds: dotnet build
userStories.notes: The backend endpoint GET /api/books/openlibrary/{isbn} already exists in BooksController.cs and OpenLibraryService.cs. The OpenLibraryBook model returns Title, Subtitle, Authors (as key refs), PublishDate, NumberOfPages, Publishers. Authors come back as key refs only (e.g. '/authors/OL1A') — use the first publisher from Publishers[] and the PublishDate for date. For author name, the OpenLibrary ISBN endpoint does not return full names directly; populate what's available and leave name fields blank if not resolvable. Wire the existing booksApi.ts or add a new lookup function.

userStories.title: Reading Status field on books
userStories.description: As a reader, I want to mark each book as 'Want to Read', 'Reading', or 'Read', so I can track my reading progress.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a ReadingStatus enum to the Book entity: WantToRead, Reading, Read
Add readingStatus column to the database via a new EF Core migration
Default value is WantToRead
Expose readingStatus in BookReadDto and BookCreateDto
Add a status dropdown/select to both the Add Book form and the Edit Book form
Display the reading status badge on the book detail page and in the book list
Status badge uses distinct colours: WantToRead = slate, Reading = amber, Read = green
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
Backend builds: dotnet build
userStories.notes: Add the enum in Models/Entities/Book.cs. Create a migration with: dotnet ef migrations add AddReadingStatus. Update BookReadDto.cs and BookCreateDto.cs. Update Mapster mappings if needed (MapsterConfig.cs). Update BooksPage reducer and BookDetailPage reducer to handle the new field. The BookForm component should get a new select field — see existing Field subcomponent pattern in BookForm.tsx.

userStories.title: Translator and Publisher fields
userStories.description: As a reader of translated literature, I want to record the translator and publisher of a book, so I can credit translators as co-authors and track small-press publishers.
acceptanceCriteria: Add translator (string, max 200, optional) and publisher (string, max 200, optional) columns to the Book entity and database via a new EF Core migration
Expose translator and publisher in BookReadDto and BookCreateDto
Add Translator and Publisher input fields to both the Add Book form and the Edit Book form
Display translator (if present) on the book detail page, labelled 'Translated by'
Display publisher (if present) on the book detail page
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
Backend builds: dotnet build
userStories.notes: Similar pattern to any other Book scalar field addition. Migration: dotnet ef migrations add AddTranslatorAndPublisher. Update BookForm.tsx to add two new text fields using the existing Field component pattern. Update the detail page display section. Update the TypeScript Book type in models/book.ts.

userStories.title: Search and filter in the book list
userStories.description: As a user with a growing collection, I want to search by title or author and filter by reading status, so I can quickly find the book I'm looking for.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a text search input above the book list on BooksPage
Search filters the displayed list client-side by title and author name (case-insensitive, partial match)
Add a reading status filter dropdown (All / Want to Read / Reading / Read) next to the search input
Search and filter work together (both active simultaneously)
When no results match, show a friendly empty state message
Search input is debounced (300ms) to avoid excessive re-renders
Filter state is local to the component — no URL persistence needed for now
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
userStories.notes: This is entirely a frontend change. The full book list is already loaded into state in BooksPage.tsx — just add a derived filtered list using useMemo. Follow the existing Elm-style reducer pattern: add SearchQueryChanged and StatusFilterChanged Msg variants. No backend changes needed. Reading status filter depends on US-002 being completed first.

userStories.title: 'Currently Reading' bookmark in the SNES bookshelf
userStories.description: As a user, I want to see a small bookmark sticking out of the spine of the book I'm currently reading in the bookshelf room, so the shelf feels alive and personal.
acceptanceCriteria: Books with readingStatus = 'Reading' display a small bookmark tab sticking up above their spine in the SNES bookshelf room
The bookmark is a bright red/crimson colour, 3px wide, 8px tall, positioned at the top of the spine
If multiple books have status Reading, all of them show a bookmark
The bookmark does not interfere with hover tooltips or click navigation
The bookmark is rendered using Kaplay primitives (rect or similar), consistent with the existing pixel-art aesthetic
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
userStories.notes: All changes are in BookshelfRoom.tsx. After the spine rect is drawn for each book, check if book.readingStatus === 'Reading' and draw an additional rect above the spine top. The Book type from the API will now include readingStatus after US-002. Use the existing Kaplay add() / rect() pattern already in the file. Keep it pixel-perfect: 3px wide, 8-10px tall, offset slightly from centre of the spine.

userStories.title: Per-book private notes
userStories.description: As a reader, I want to write private freeform notes on each book, so I can record personal reactions, memories, and observations that are just for me.
acceptanceCriteria: Add a notes column (text, optional) to the Book entity and database via a new EF Core migration
Expose notes in BookReadDto and BookCreateDto
Add a multiline textarea for notes in the Edit Book form on BookDetailPage
Display notes (if present) on the book detail page under a 'My Notes' heading
Notes are not shown in the book list — detail page only
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
Backend builds: dotnet build
userStories.notes: Simple scalar text field addition — same pattern as description. Migration: dotnet ef migrations add AddBookNotes. Add a <textarea> in BookDetailPage edit form using the existing Field component pattern. Render notes in the read view beneath description. No markdown rendering needed at this stage — plain text is fine.

userStories.title: Day/night cycle in the SNES bookshelf room
userStories.description: As a user, I want the bookshelf room to reflect the time of day — morning light, golden afternoon, moonlit night — so the shelf feels alive and grounded in my real day.
acceptanceCriteria: The bookshelf room reads the current local time when the scene loads
06:00–11:59 → morning: warm light tint on the room, pale blue sky through the window
12:00–16:59 → afternoon: neutral room lighting, bright white-yellow sky
17:00–20:59 → evening/golden hour: amber/orange tint on walls and floor, warm sky
21:00–05:59 → night: dark navy room overlay, moon visible through the window
The tint is applied as a semi-transparent colour overlay on the background sprite, not by recolouring individual elements
No real-time tick required — time is sampled once on scene load
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
userStories.notes: All changes in BookshelfRoom.tsx and/or snesRoom.ts. After buildRoomBackground() renders the base scene, use Kaplay's rect() with a low-opacity colour fill over the full canvas (440×270) to apply the tint. Read time via new Date().getHours(). The window area in snesRoom.ts is already drawn — optionally also change the window colour to match the sky palette. Keep the overlay opacity subtle (0.10–0.20 alpha) so the room detail stays visible.

userStories.title: Custom named shelves (collections)
userStories.description: As a reader, I want to organise my books into named collections beyond the default shelf — such as 'Abandoned', 'To Reread', 'Books That Broke Me' — so I can curate my library my way.
acceptanceCriteria: REFINEMENT NEEDED: Decide whether collections are a separate entity (many-to-many with books) or a simple tag/label on each book
REFINEMENT NEEDED: Decide how collections appear in the SNES bookshelf — as separate tab/shelf-switch, or as a filter on the existing room
REFINEMENT NEEDED: Decide the UX for adding/removing a book from a collection (from list, from detail page, or both)
A book can belong to multiple collections simultaneously
Collections can be created, renamed, and deleted by the user
The default view (no collection selected) shows all books as today
userStories.notes: This is the most architecturally significant Phase 2 feature. Before implementing, a product decision is needed: (A) A Collection entity with a many-to-many join to books — clean but heavier; or (B) A simple comma-separated 'shelf' tag field on Book, similar to how genres currently work — lower effort but less flexible. The SNES bookshelf integration is also open: does navigating collections mean switching scenes in Kaplay, or just re-filtering which books are rendered? Recommend starting with option B (tag-based) and the filter approach — avoids premature DB complexity. Do not implement until the refinement questions are resolved.

userStories.title: Cover art from OpenLibrary and dominant-colour spine tinting
userStories.description: As a user, I want my books to display their cover art and have their SNES spine colour automatically derived from the cover, so my shelf looks unmistakably mine.
acceptanceCriteria: When an ISBN is present, fetch the cover image URL from OpenLibrary Covers API: https://covers.openlibrary.org/b/isbn/{isbn}-M.jpg
Store coverUrl (string, optional) on the Book entity via a new EF Core migration
Display the cover image on the book detail page if coverUrl is present
In the SNES bookshelf room, if a book has a coverUrl, extract the dominant colour from the image and use it as the spine colour instead of the random palette colour
Dominant colour extraction runs client-side using a hidden <canvas> element — no external library needed
If cover fetch fails or no ISBN, fall back to the existing random palette colour — no errors shown to the user
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
Backend builds: dotnet build
userStories.notes: OpenLibrary cover URL format: https://covers.openlibrary.org/b/isbn/{isbn}-M.jpg (M = medium). No API key needed. To extract dominant colour client-side: draw the image onto a hidden <canvas>, call getImageData(), average the RGB values of non-white/non-black pixels. Store the result as a hex string or use it transiently in the Kaplay scene. The coverUrl can be auto-populated when the ISBN lookup (US-001) runs, or added manually. Backend: add CoverUrl string field to Book entity and DTOs. The BookshelfRoom.tsx spine-colour logic currently uses a deterministic palette array — replace the colour lookup for books that have a coverUrl.

userStories.title: Bulk import from Goodreads CSV export
userStories.description: As a user migrating from Goodreads, I want to upload my Goodreads export CSV and have my existing library imported into BookWorm, so I don't have to re-enter hundreds of books manually.
acceptanceCriteria: REFINEMENT NEEDED: Decide where the import UI lives — dedicated page, modal on BooksPage, or settings area
REFINEMENT NEEDED: Decide handling of duplicates — skip silently, overwrite, or prompt the user
REFINEMENT NEEDED: Confirm which Goodreads CSV columns map to which BookWorm fields (Title, Author, ISBN, Bookshelves → readingStatus, Date Read → finishedAt, My Rating → rating if US-016 is done)
A file input accepts a .csv file
Parsed rows are POSTed to /api/books in sequence or batch
A progress indicator shows import status (X of N books imported)
Rows that fail validation are reported to the user with row number and reason — import continues for valid rows
Frontend typechecks pass: cd ClientApp && npm run build
Backend builds: dotnet build
userStories.notes: Goodreads CSV columns of interest: Title, Author, ISBN, ISBN13, My Rating, Number of Pages, Year Published, Date Read, Date Added, Bookshelves, Exclusive Shelf (read/currently-reading/to-read). The Exclusive Shelf column maps cleanly to ReadingStatus. Consider implementing the CSV parse entirely on the frontend (FileReader API + manual CSV split) to avoid a new backend endpoint. Papa Parse is a popular CSV library but check if adding a dependency is acceptable — alternatively a simple manual split works for well-formed CSVs. Do not implement until refinement questions are resolved and US-002 and US-003 are complete.
